feedback.text: Moving to an increasingly digital future, I would like to raise awareness, first and foremost, to the increasingly invasive and cartel-like practices of the two major payment processing companies, Visa and MasterCard. They have, time and time again, proven that they are more than happy to use their denial of services, services that are essentially, nowadays, mandatory if any business gets to survive, especially online, based on morals or their brands' 'risk' assessment. Considering there is no major alternative for payment processing they are in fact a monopoly and brand image has nothing to do with risk with no competition and a payment processing company has no rights dictating what an adult can buy with his own money if the transaction is legal. It happened with physical legal media of adult content in Japan some years ago leading to the shutdown of many businesses with no recourse. Coming to today they again implemented a censorship mandate on the biggest digital platform for selling video games, namely Steam. After pressure by an Australian activist group that wants to dictate how and for what content an adult will spend their money, Visa and MasterCard threatened to remove their services if content with adult themes are not removed from the marketplace. This sets a precedent and is a slippery slope for suffocating censorship and market control never before seen in an increasingly interconnected online world. Continuing on video games, which is a relatively new medium of art considering it is a combination of music, cinematography, animation etc, and digital media as a whole. I would like to point at a concerning gray area of digital goods, on the fact that purchasing is not truly owning. There needs to be a contingency plan for people that purchase online digital goods and their right of access of said purchase even after the end of life of said digital goods. In a crude example: much like if someone purchases a car, a car company won't deactivate said car, because the model is discontinued. There is an initiative called Stop Killing Games that goes into details on the matter and is an important part not only for video games but for digital transactions in general. Building from the ground up moving forward, it will be possible even for live service games and digital goods to be able to be run by a community of people privately on private servers, with barely any cost to any company. These are in my eyes the most urgent matters to attend to considering the rapid evolution of online transactions. Thank you for your time.
